label: a
text_statement: Explain what is meant by the accuracy of a measured value.

label: b(i)
text_statement: Two solid cubes, A and B, are measured to determine the density of their materials. Table 1.1 shows the measurements for cube A. Show that the calculated density of the material of cube A is 8.7 × 10^3 kg/m^3.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: Calculate the percentage uncertainty in the density of the material of cube A.

label: b(iii)
text_statement: The density of the material of cube B is determined to be 9.2 × 10^3 kg/m^3 ± 6%. State and explain whether cube A and cube B could be made from the same material.

label: NULL
text_statement: Moments and Upthrust

label: a
text_statement: State the principle of moments.

label: b(i)
text_statement: A solid plastic cylinder floats in water. It is used to support one end of a horizontal uniform beam AB as shown in Fig. 2.1. The beam has length 6.0 m and weight 1700 N. The beam is attached to solid ground with a hinge at end A. The cylinder applies a vertical force of 1300 N to the beam. A person of weight 660 N stands on the beam at point P. The beam AB is in equilibrium. By taking moments about end A, determine the distance x from A to P.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: The bottom of the cylinder is submerged in the water to depth y as shown in Fig. 2.2. The cylinder has mass 11 kg and diameter 0.78 m. The beam exerts a vertical force of 1300 N on the cylinder. The cylinder is in equilibrium. Show that the upthrust acting on the cylinder is 1400 N.

label: b(iii)
text_statement: Calculate the depth y. The water has density 990 kg/m^3.

label: b(iv)
text_statement: The person can stand anywhere between A and B. On Fig. 2.3, sketch the variation of the depth of the bottom of the cylinder with the distance of the person from A, for distances between 0 and 6.0 m. Numerical values are not required.

label: NULL
text_statement: Dynamics and Energy

label: a(i)
text_statement: A truck R of mass 9400 kg moves with constant acceleration in a straight line down a slope, as illustrated in Fig. 3.1. At point A the speed of the truck is 13 m/s and at point B the speed of the truck is 22 m/s. A and B are a distance of 180 m apart. Calculate the acceleration of the truck between A and B.

label: a(ii)
text_statement: Determine the gain in kinetic energy of the truck between A and B.

label: b(i)
text_statement: Define force.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: A short time after passing point B truck R moves in a straight line on horizontal ground. The driver of the truck applies the brakes. Fig. 3.2 shows the variation with time of the momentum of the truck. Show that the average resultant force F acting on truck R between time t = 0 and t = 15 s is –1.2 × 10^4 N.

label: b(iii)
text_statement: An identical truck S has the same initial momentum as truck R. Truck S experiences a constant force equal to the force F in (b)(ii). State and explain whether truck S will take more, less or the same amount of time to come to rest as truck R.

label: NULL
text_statement: Waves and Stationary Waves

label: a
text_statement: A device containing a microwave emitter and receiver is placed in front of a large metal sheet in a vacuum as shown in Fig. 4.1. The line XY is perpendicular to the metal sheet. The device emits microwaves of frequency 6.3 GHz. When the device is at position P, a stationary wave is formed between the device and the sheet. Explain how the stationary wave, including the nodes and the antinodes, is formed.

label: b(i)
text_statement: Calculate the wavelength of the microwaves.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: At point P the receiver detects a maximum amplitude of the stationary wave. The device is moved slowly from point P along the line XY and the receiver detects a series of minimum and maximum amplitudes. The first time a minimum amplitude is detected by the receiver is when the device is at point Q. Determine the distance between P and Q.

label: b(iii)
text_statement: The intensity of the microwaves emitted by the device is increased. The frequency of the microwaves is unchanged. The device is moved slowly along the line XY from point Q until the next maximum amplitude is detected at point R. State and explain whether the distance QR is greater than, less than or the same as distance PQ.

label: NULL
text_statement: Doppler Effect

label: a
text_statement: A stationary loudspeaker emits sound of constant frequency. A microphone is placed near to the loudspeaker and connected to a cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO). The trace on the screen of the CRO is shown in Fig. 5.1. The time-base of the CRO is set to 5.0 × 10^-4 s/cm. The speed of the sound emitted by the loudspeaker is 330 m/s. Determine the wavelength of the sound.

label: b
text_statement: The loudspeaker now moves in a straight line while emitting the same sound of constant frequency. The period of the trace on the CRO increases continuously. Describe the motion of the loudspeaker.

label: NULL
text_statement: Electricity and Drift Speed

label: a
text_statement: A wire has resistance 3.3 mΩ. The current in the wire is 0.85 A. Calculate the potential difference across the wire.

label: b(i)
text_statement: The wire has length 0.24 m and is made of copper of resistivity 1.8 × 10^-8 Ω·m. Show that the cross-sectional area of the wire is 1.3 × 10^-6 m^2.

label: b(ii)
text_statement: The total number of free electrons in the wire is 2.6 × 10^22. Determine the number density n of the free electrons in the copper.

label: b(iii)
text_statement: Calculate the average drift speed of the free electrons in the wire.

label: c(i)
text_statement: A second wire Q, also made of copper, is used. Wire Q has the same resistance as wire P. Wire Q has a cross-sectional area that decreases uniformly from one end to the other. Explain why the resistance of wire Q is greater than the resistance of wire P.

label: c(ii)
text_statement: Sketch a graph to show the variation of the average drift speed of the free electrons in wire Q with distance from the end with the larger cross-sectional area. Numerical values are not required.

label: NULL
text_statement: Particle Physics

label: a
text_statement: A nucleus of radium-226 (226 88 Ra) decays into a nucleus of radon (Rn) and an alpha particle. Nucleon number of Rn = ..., Proton number of Rn = ...

label: b
text_statement: The alpha particle is emitted with a speed of 1.5 × 10^7 m/s. The radon nucleus is initially at rest. Calculate the speed of the radon nucleus after the decay.

label: c
text_statement: State three quantities, other than mass-energy, that are conserved in the decay.
